registry.description: Peace is the condition of wholeness, right relationship, and rest — the Hebrew shalom covering far more than the absence of conflict. It means completeness, prosperity, welfare, the flourishing of everything and everyone in their proper place. It is used of the end of hostilities, of the quality of a life well-lived, of the relationship between a person and God when that relationship is restored. The New Testament declares that Christ himself is our peace: through him the fundamental rupture between humanity and God is healed. Peace is the signature of the kingdom.

groups.context_description: Term names the relational orientation of Israel toward God — the shift from servile master to intimate husband naming the transformation of inner relationship
Term names divine blessing as the inner-dispositioned gift of God — the overflow of God's favour toward persons, bringing flourishing, peace, and life
Term names the inner act of bestowing blessing — the patriarch, leader, or friend invoking divine favour upon another as an act of love, covenant, or relationship
Wellbeing expressed in greeting — the inner orientation of goodwill and wholeness directed toward another through formal invocation of peace
groups.notes: Revised during Dimension Review Phase B.5: characteristic-perspective rewrite. Old: Term names the formal salutation of peace — the invocation of wholeness and wellbeing at the opening of official correspondence

groups.context_description: Term names the inner disposition of hushed stillness — silence as judgment's response or as the posture of patient waiting for divine salvation
Term names the cessation or ruination of the inner person — the collapse of human dignity, prophetic undoing before holiness, or unceasing grief
Term names the inner state of stillness or its absence — being motionless before God in awe, patient waiting, or unceasing distress
Term names the inner act of becoming or keeping silent — the stilling of the self before God, in grief, in reverence, or in wise restraint
Term names the stillness from which the divine voice is heard — the profound inner and outer silence that attends divine self-disclosure
Term names the granting of relief — a remission that brings rest and lightening of burden to those who receive it
Term names the commanded inner silence — the hush of reverence, awe, or holy restraint before God or in the face of his coming
Term names the inner devising of evil — the heart plotting harm or crafting schemes against another
Term names the metaphorical plowing of iniquity — inner sin etched deeply into the heart or expressed as a pattern of moral cultivation
Term names the posture of inner restraint — holding silence in response to emotional provocation, injustice, or the desire to speak
Term names the receptive posture of silence — an inner orientation of attentive submission, readiness to hear or receive
Term names the posture of wisdom — silence as an expression of inner discernment, understanding, or moral restraint
Term names the inner condition when an appeal is made for God not to be silent — the psalmist's existential need for divine responsiveness
Term names God's own posture of silence or its breaking — divine inner restraint, patience, or deliberate withholding
Term applied to ethical-existential confrontation — the moral weight of remaining silent at a critical moment of injustice or crisis
Term names the inward suppression of speech under emotional or spiritual pressure, with deteriorating inner consequence
Term names the moral dimension of silence — the choice to be silent or not at a moment of ethical weight
Term names God's posture of silence and its breaking — divine restraint, patience, or resolve
Term names prophetic inner resolve — refusing silence in advocacy for God's purposes
Term names a state of existential restlessness — the absence of resting-place as the condition of the whole person, particularly in exile or distress
Term names the soul's own rest — the inner condition of peace, security, and settled orientation toward God
Term names the covenantal rest given by God to his people — a condition of settled security, peace, and inheritance promised and fulfilled
Term names an eschatological or divine resting-place — where God dwells, and the orientation of his own will and delight
Term names rest as the condition of the soul that walks in the right way — an inner-being orientation of peace attained through faithful obedience
Term names divine repose offered to the weary — the condition of inner rest available through God's provision
Term names the Spirit resting upon a person — divine inner-being impartation resulting in transformed capacities
Term names God's gift of rest to his people — the settled condition of security, peace, and wholeness bestowed as covenant blessing
Term names the inner restlessness or rest of the human person — the condition of the inner life in the absence or presence of peace
Term names wisdom, anger, or another inner quality resting in a place within the person
Term names God's own inner rest, restraint, satisfaction, or calm — the inner orientation of God expressed through this root
Term names the call or appeal not to be abandoned by God — the inner-being distress of one fearing divine withdrawal
Term names the dwelling of God's presence as an inner-oriented rest-place, where his people find salvation and joy
Term names relief as a condition of the inner life freed from threat
Term names quietness as an inner-being condition of peace, receptivity to wisdom, and strength before God
Term names the soothing/pleasing quality of sacrifice as a relational act — the inner-being dimension of worship as something God receives with pleasure, establishing a condition of divine acceptance
Term names God's inner response to the aroma — the explicit disclosure of God's heart orientation through the receiving or withholding of acceptance
Term names the misdirected inner-being act of worship — the offering of pleasing aroma to idols as an expression of unfaithful inner orientation
Term names a signal or banner that orients the inner life — the object to which hope, fear, or trust is directed, or from which the inner life flees in panic
Term names the jealousy of God as a defining inner characteristic — God's exclusive passionate claim on his people's worship and loyalty
Term names God's disturbing/stirring power in creation — an expression of divine inner capacity and sovereignty
Term names the rest or respite sought or denied — the inner-being condition of peace in exile, distress, or covenant promise
Term names the restful inner posture of the peaceable person — the quality of quietness as a character orientation, in contrast to those who devise deceit
Term names the inner condition of complacent ease — the self-secure, untroubled disposition that produces contempt, moral blindness, or judgment
Term names the divinely intended condition of secure quietness — the ease of God's people living in peace and safety as a condition of shalom
Term names the inner condition of ease and security — the state of the person wholly undisturbed, here in the context of unjust prosperity
Term names the inner condition of quietness, rest, and trust as the spiritual posture of the person before God — the condition from which strength and salvation flow
Term names the inner restlessness of the wicked or troubled person — the inability to be quiet as an expression of inner moral or emotional disorder
Term names the quiet of God — God's own inner posture of watchful stillness, or the breaking of it in response to his people's need or his own resolve
Term names the promised rest of God's people — the condition of quiet and ease as covenant blessing, contrasted with restlessness under judgment
Term names quietness as a condition of divinely bestowed peace — the character of a reign and its people under God's gift of rest
Term names relief from pressure or affliction — the inner-being experience of rest when burdens are lifted, or its absence when they persist
Term names the inner disposition and active practice of relational peace — the will directed toward harmony with others
Term names the inner-being peace given by God through Christ — the reconciled state before God and the interior experience of divine peace guarding the heart and mind
Term names peace as apostolic blessing and greeting — the invocation of divine shalom upon persons in the community of Christ
Term names peace as the eschatological and relational fruit of the kingdom — achieved by Christ, to be pursued in community and received as the Spirit's gift
Term names peaceableness as an inner character quality — the disposition of the trained person or the wisdom from above, producing righteous fruit
Term names the act of making peace — Christ's reconciling work that restores the broken relationship between humanity and God
Term names peaceful quietude as an inner-being quality of life — the disposition of undisturbed, godly calm in social context
Term names the inner disposition of settling into quiet — the ceasing of noise, argument, or resistance, whether in reverence, acceptance, or godly aspiration
Term names a disposition of quiet attentiveness or ordered restraint — the inner quality of settled quiet that enables learning, work, or worship
Term names a gentle, quiet spirit as an inner-being quality — the disposition of settled peace within, precious in God's sight
Term names the inner discipline of silence — the restraint of voice in reverence, receptiveness, or ordered worship
Term names profound silence as an inner-being atmosphere — the hush of awe before a significant moment
Term names the inner state and act of silence — holding the voice still in the presence of power, shame, or reverence
Term names the silencing of voice or argument — the constraint of expression, whether as shame, spiritual authority, or the witness of righteous action

peace, harmony, tranquility; 
peace, Lk. 14:32; Acts 12:20; tranquillity, safety, welfare, health; often with an emphasis on lack of strife or reconciliation in a relation, as when one has "peace with God." This word generally follows the meanings and usage of H8934; peace, Lk. 14:32; Acts 12:20; tranquillity, Lk. 11:21; Jn. 16:33; 1Thess. 5:3; concord, unity, love of peace, harmony Mt. 10:34; Lk. 12:51; meton. the author of peace, Eph. 2:14; from the Hebrew every kind of blessing and good, Lk. 1:79; 2:14, 29; metonymy a salutation expressive of good wishes, a benediction, blessing, Mt. 10:13

to make peace, to cause reconciliation between two parties, as in Christ causing the believer’s peace with God reconcile 
to make peace, Col. 1:20*

to be silent, have no objection; to rest; to lead a quiet life; to give up

session_b_findings.finding: The peace registry's inner-being content is unusually wide — spanning the felt inner state of rest/peace (Emotion — Positive), the inner posture of quietness before God (Moral Character/Relational Disposition), the divine giving of rest (Transformation/GOD), and God's own inner states of rest, restraint, and jealousy. Session B should examine what shalom names as an inner-being reality: is it primarily a felt state, a relational condition, a divine gift, or an eschatological reality — and how do these dimensions interrelate?
